data.title: S&P 500 Snapshot: 4-Day Win Streak Snapped
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

The S&P 500 posted four straight days of gains before inching down on Friday. The index ultimately finished the week up 1.6%, marking its fifth weekly gain in the past six weeks. Here is a snapshot of the index from the past week:

The table below summarizes the number of record highs reached each year dating back to 2013.

Here is a snapshot of the index from the past six months with a 50-day moving average:

S&P 500: A Perspective on Drawdowns

On October 9, 2007, the S&P 500 reached an all-time high, closing the day at 1565.15. Then on March 9, 2009, the index dropped ~57% off of its high from exactly 17 months before, closing the day at 676.53. This time period became known as the Global Financial Crisis. It took over 5 years before the index reached a new all-time high on March 28, 2013, where it closed out at 1569.19. The chart below is a snapshot of record highs and selloffs since the 2007 peak reached on October 9, 2007.

What happens if we take out the Global Financial Crisis? Here's a snapshot of the same chart above where the start date has been changed to the trough reached on March 9, 2009. Note the recent selloffs in 2022.

Here are a few tables with the number of days of a 1% or greater change in either direction and the number of days of corrections (down 10% or more from the record high).

And here is a linear chart of the index since October 9, 2007:

Here is a linearly scaled version of the same chart with the 50- and 200-day moving averages. The index has been above the 50-day moving average since May 1st and above the 200-day moving average since May 12th. Additionally, the 50-day moving average has been above the 200-day moving average since July 1st.

S&P 500: A Perspective on Volatility

For a sense of the correlation between the closing price and intraday volatility, the chart below overlays the S&P 500 since 2007 with the intraday price range. On April 9th, the index experienced its largest intraday price volatility (10.77%) since December 24th, 2018 (19.10%). Also included is the 20-day moving average to identify trends in volatility. Over the past 20 days, the average percent change from the intraday low to the intraday high is 0.71%.

S&P 500 versus S&P Equal Weight

The S&P 500 is a market cap-weighted index, which includes roughly the 500 largest U.S. stocks spanning 11 sectors. The S&P 500 Equal Weight Index includes the same constituents as the S&P 500, but each company is equally weighted at a fixed weight. So how do these two indexes match up against each other this year?

The S&P 500 is currently up 12.20% year-to-date, while the S&P Equal Weight is up 7.81% year-to-date.

ETFs associated with the S&P 500 include: iShares Core S&P 500 ETF (IVV), SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (SPY), Vanguard S&P 500 ETF (VOO), SPDR Portfolio S&P 500 ETF (SPLG), and Invesco S&P 500® Equal Weight ETF (RSP).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Wrapping Up The 2024 SPIVA Institutional Scorecard
data.description:
By Michael Brower

For over 20 years, the S&P Indices versus Active (SPIVA®) U.S. Scorecard has assessed how active mutual fund managers perform against their relevant S&P Dow Jones Indices benchmarks across various timeframes and asset classes. The scorecard’s methodology was enhanced in 2015 to include institutional accounts and was further extended in 2023 to include separately managed wrap accounts (SMAs), which have an annual fee structure that bundles (or wraps) all the administrative, commission and management expenses for the account.

Wrap accounts differ from institutionally focused separate accounts in that they allow smaller market participants to access professional portfolio managers, which were once only available to large institutional investors.1 The SPIVA Institutional Scorecard is important because it facilitates comparisons between institutional and SMA/wrap accounts on a gross-of-fees basis, eliminating any possibility that fees are the sole contributor to a given manager’s underperformance.

Exhibit 1 demonstrates that 55% of equity SMAs/wrap accounts underperformed their benchmarks in 2024, down 5% from 2023. This difference was more pronounced in fixed income, where only 32% of SMAs/wrap accounts underperformed in 2024, but over 65% underperformed in the year prior. Nevertheless, and consistent with past SPIVA research, rates of underperformance for equities and fixed income increased over longer measurement periods. 80% of equity accounts and 57% of fixed income accounts failed to outperform their respective benchmarks over a 10-year period.

Active performance varied considerably by market capitalization. 67% of All Large-Cap SMA/wrap managers underperformed the S&P 500® in 2024, generally consistent with their institutional mutual fund peers. Small-cap managers fared better, with only 19% underperforming their respective benchmarks in 2024, outpacing the 30% underperformance rate in 2023. Managers were perhaps aided by style bias opportunities to tilt toward outperforming large-cap stocks. Last year, The 500™ outpaced the S&P MidCap 400® and S&P SmallCap 600® by 11.1% and 16.3%, respectively.

Turning to fixed income SMA/wrap performance, Exhibit 3 shows that U.S. Aggregate and Core accounts delivered majority outperformance, with only 11% and 13% of accounts failing to outperform their benchmarks, respectively, which was an improvement over their institutional peers. However, Municipal accounts may have had a tougher time, with more than half underperforming the S&P National AMT-Free Municipal Bond Index.

Beating the benchmark can be a challenge regardless of investment vehicle, and SMAs/Wrap accounts are no exception. For a more complete analysis of performance across segregated institutional and SMAs/wrap accounts, we invite you to read our full 2024 SPIVA Institutional Scorecard.

1 Comprehensive definitions for SMAs can be found in the eVestment Alliance Glossary of Terms.

Disclosure: Copyright © 2025 S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC, a division of S&P Global. All rights reserved. This material is reproduced with the prior written consent of S&P DJI. For more information on S&P DJI, please visit www.spdji.com. For full terms of use and disclosures, please visit www.spdji.com/terms-of-use.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Politics And The Markets 09/13/25
data.description:
This is the forum for daily political discussion on Seeking Alpha. A new version is published every market day.

Please don't leave political comments on other articles or posts on the site.

The comments below are not regulated with the same rigor as the rest of the site, and this is an 'enter at your own risk' area as discussion can get very heated. If you can't stand the heat... you know what they say...

More on Today's Markets:

Moderation Guidelines:

We remove comments under the following categories:

  • Personal attacks on another user account
  • Anti-Vaxxer or covid related misinformation
  • Stereotyping, prejudiced or racist language about individuals or the topic under discussion.
  • Inciting violence messages, encouraging hate groups and political violence.

Regardless of which side of the political divide you find yourself, please be courteous and don't direct abuse at other users.

For any issue with regards to comments please email us at : moderation@seekingalpha.com.

Seeking Alpha's Disclosure: Past performance is no guarantee of future results. No recommendation or advice is being given as to whether any investment is suitable for a particular investor. Any views or opinions expressed above may not reflect those of Seeking Alpha as a whole. Seeking Alpha is not a licensed securities dealer, broker or US investment adviser or investment bank. Our analysts are third party authors that include both professional investors and individual investors who may not be licensed or certified by any institute or regulatory body.

data.title: Expand Energy: Option Contracts Beat Both LNG And AI
data.description:
Thesis

Expand Energy's (NASDAQ:EXE) stock price is down 10% since I last evaluated the company in early April. This follows a 25% decline in the natural gas commodity price (NG1:COM) during the seasonal inventory build.

Inventory levels for this time of year mirror 2024, indicating stronger production exiting the winter burn down period. Despite the higher than anticipated build rate, the 2026 strip appears constructive thanks to a steady ramp in LNG capacity. This steady demand ramp along with increased power demand to supply AI infrastructure provides a bullish outlook for the natural gas industry.

Despite solid long-term fundamentals and an improved valuation, I re-iterate my HOLD rating. My defensive stance derives from a commodity whose usage remains heavily dominated by the weather. Natural gas markets are structurally more equipped to handle winter demand than the previous winter.

Today I introduce the concept of using puts and covered calls to capitalize on seasonal price swings. Current option contract offerings can yield over 10% before year end and peak winter demand. In a game of rock-paper-scissors, pre-winter options beats both AI and LNG exports.

Update To My Previous Coverage

In April, I detailed why I felt EXE was fairly valued for a mid-cycle price of $3.75/mcf. I expected the company to produce $2.3 billion in free cash flow for the year at this price deck. So far, this has panned out to be fairly accurate with the company reporting $1.2 billion in FCF through the first six months of the year.

However, despite the solid performance, the stock is now down 10%, trending alongside the weaker commodity prices. This weakness did not dampen the second quarter's results, as EXE reported a $877 million gain through hedge derivatives that provided a floor of $3.39/MCF during the quarter. The downside protection turned out to be a large boost, and allowed the company to report a $4.02/share profit.

Currently, the company still appears to be benefiting from its hedge book, as it has roughly 4 BCF/d of production hedged with a $3.41/MCF floor. Natural gas prices have been at or below this level since April and therefore should be a net benefit in Q3. This should help EXE again report positive earnings as we enter the final weeks of the seasonal inventory build.

The company has also reported moderate operating and capital efficiency improvements on its way to boosting free cash flow generation in 2026. While these improvements are material, their impact is greatly overshadowed by the tax savings introduced through the One Big Beautiful Bill.

The company expects to realize tax savings of $150 million in 2025 and a subsequent $400 million in 2026. Combined, the operational improvements and tax savings are allowing EXE to return even more cash to its shareholders through both equity and dividends.

After repurchasing $100 million in common stock during the quarter, the company is also supporting the stock price by continuing to drive down debt. The new cash flow profile is allowing the company to paydown an additional $500 million in debt, improving shareholder equity while also resulting in some interest savings.

Shareholders are also getting a bit more cash this quarter with a $0.89 variable dividend paid in addition to the base dividend in September. This represents the first variable dividend the company has paid since the second quarter of 2023. If this performance continues, EXE could turn into a solid high yield play, but I'd like to see how management approaches this in the next several quarters.

Why I Am Still Playing Defense For Natural Gas Producers

In my previous article, I discussed how EXE could likely achieve a 10% FCF yield at an annual $3.75/mcf strip. That number has slightly improved today thanks to the lower share price and previously discussed efficiency gains.

When I started working on this article, I first wanted to decide if the current price discount was enough to justify an upgrade to a buy recommendation. From a valuation standpoint, EXE has seen the best improvement in relative price among its natural gas producing peers. By itself this seems like it should be enough to warrant an upgrade.

However, the macro environment for natural gas is what still makes me hesitant to take a long-term position. The 2025 winter is very strong from a demand perspective, particularly in comparison to 2023 and 2024. As shown below in EIA storage data, the winter burn off was able to deplete inventories by an additional 700 BCF compared to the prior year.

That winter was set up very constructively. On the heels of two straight warmer than average winters, capital investment into natural gas basins waned significantly. This was necessary to weather the $2/mcf environment that persisted for much of 2024.

Producers apparently were ready for a return to normal, with many private companies' drilling and completing wells just in advance of the winter season. This additional activity has allowed inventories to essentially normalize with August of 2024. The math works out to mean that US production has outpaced the 2024 inventory build season by around 1.5 BCF/d.

Simply put, natural gas supply is on better footing leading into winter 2026 than we were this time last year.

More importantly, the biggest takeaway from this chart is the dependency on weather for demand of the commodity. There's no denying that soft winter weather led to the price declines in 2024. However, many look to AI to provide steady baseload demand through the build out of natural gas power plants.

However, the difference in inventory burndown between winter 2024 and 2025 is around 6 BCF/d. That's a big demand draw that is not easily replicated nor replaced in just a few short months (not to mention less demand for LNG). A weak winter is something that markets can't predict, yet the impact can result in an oversupplied market for 12-18 months.

Option Contracts Provide Better Value Than Long-Term Ownership

Despite my pessimism for long-term ownership, I still see the ability to extract profits out of natural gas providers using options. We have an interesting set up where the immediate tone is bearish due to the weaker commodity price but still a bullish outlook due to the impending winter demand. For this reason, I like the December contracts as they are early enough to capture the anticipation to winter, but not late enough to declare the winter a bust.

As of this writing, EXE is trading at $96.50/share with mid-December call options available for roughly $4.70 and $2.35/share at strike prices of $100 and $105/share respectively. Even though the delta is around 0.4, I think this presents good value.

Using the $105 strike price as an example, this transaction would generate $10.85/share profit for only three months of ownership. Adding in a dividend payment along the way (early December payout) paves a clear way to generating a 12%+ return in just three months. Further value can be added if shares are bought via a $95 dollar put.

In the worst-case scenario, the stock never hits the $105 strike price this winter or even declines. Under these conditions, my effective cost basis is roughly $94/share on a stock that is already producing a 10% FCF yield. I also see comparable options available for the peer group (RRC), (EQT), and (AR).

Risks

I've already discussed the weather-related risks to natural gas demand. Option trading also has its own set of risks as well. Understanding the differences between normal stock trading and option trading is key for anyone interested in this strategy, even though I feel it is well suited for a seasonal commodity.

I would also not advise anyone to use leverage to write a call option. EXE is a solid company that trades at a fair, if not a slightly undervalued, valuation. I would feel comfortable being forced to hold the stock if the call option never manifests. It certainly can find a home in my portfolio, and in this sense, I see little need to use debt to acquire the 100 shares necessary for an options contract.

The use of options also limits the potential upside to owning a given stock. To give an example, if EXE were to rise to $110, if a $105 strike price was used for the contract, $5/share would have been left on the table (although you would also have not collected on the contract premium).

Options also commit your stock holding until the contract either executes or expires. Until then, the position cannot be liquidated unless the contract is bought back. The pros and cons of option trading may not fit everyone's investment criteria or needs, so it's worth taking the time to consider all of the variables.

Investor Key Takeaways

1. EXE reported solid Q2 earnings, in part due to a strong hedge profile that allowed the company to mitigate weakness in the commodity price.

2. Tax savings from the OBBB and operational efficiency improvements will allow EXE to generate an additional $425 million in FCF in 2025. The company is using this to retire an additional $500 million in debt.

3. Debt retirement, share repurchases, and the return of a variable dividend are all methods of improving shareholder value. If the variable dividend continues, EXE could enter the high-yield territory.

4. Despite the positives, I am cautious when approaching natural gas markets due to their reliance on weather for both the winter burn down and the summer cooling seasons. I may be too skeptical, but I don't like my capital tied up in how the weather performs.

5. The cyclicality of natural gas can still be used for profit through covered calls that are currently offered at attractive terms. Investors can generate 10% returns in just three months, creating market-beating performance.

6. If the covered call is not executed, I still view the stock as a HOLD based on a reasonable valuation and FCF yield in excess of 10% at a mid-cycle price deck of $3.75/mcf. This gives me confidence to recommend ownership if the contract does not execute as planned.

data.title: ULTY: The New Strategy Has Worked, But Conditions May Be Shifting
data.description:
We can always count on the Wall Street marketing machine to conjure up and produce funds that hit on emerging themes. Option-income products are, of course, extremely popular right now. Financial technology and efficiency gains make strategies such as covered call writing and buffered funds simple to construct and bring to the market. They can be appropriate for some investors, but I am growing increasingly concerned about the proliferation.

Enter the YieldMax Ultra Option Income Strategy ETF (NYSEARCA:ULTY). The fund is less than two years old, and it has already undergone key objective changes. And guess what? They seem to be working if you eye total return charts over the past, say, six months. Below, I compared ULTY to what I consider to be appropriate equity benchmarks-the Nasdaq 100 ETF (QQQ) and Cathie Wood's ARK Innovation ETF (ARKK).

Through direct ownership of stocks as part of the latest strategy, and buying collars (selling calls, buying puts), ULTY has returned 32% since mid-March, outperforming QQQ's 23%. Of course, the risk management process has not kept up with ARKK's torrid 57% total return.

I have a hold rating on the product. So long as prospective buyers understand the ETF's objectives, it can be appropriate. I urge inexperienced investors and those solely looking at the dividend yield to seek additional guidance, however.

Let's flesh out this fund in more detail.

ULTY: Outperforming QQQ, Underperforming ARKK Last 6 Months

ULTY: Recent Weekly Distributions

According to the issuer, ULTY is an actively managed ETF that seeks current income and that seeks exposure to the share price of select U.S.-listed securities, subject to a limit on potential investment gains. The fund's strategy involves: (i) constructing a portfolio of U.S.-listed equity securities and (2) generating income through an options portfolio, which involves using options contracts on underlying securities. The fund will also maintain a minor allocation to cash or U.S. Treasuries, not exceeding ten percent of its total assets.

ULTY is a medium-sized ETF with $3.1 billion in assets under management as of September 11, 2025. Its annual expense ratio is very high at 1.3%. Compared to other option-income funds, that's a high price to pay. The trailing 12-month dividend yield is extremely high at 119%, per Seeking Alpha.

According to the issuer, the latest distribution rate is 87.42%. In past months, the net yield has been comprised of a significant return of capital, while the most recent distribution on August 29, 2025, contained 12.82% return of capital and 87.18% income.

The new construction and strategy entails the fund owning shares of stock, while writing calls and buying puts, replacing the previous strategy of creating synthetic equity positions. Before that change earlier this year, the manager moved to collar selling strategies to limit downside risk.

Today, 15-30 "ARKKish" (tech-related, high-growth companies) compose the equity sleeve. The issuer targets stocks with high implied volatility to earn the highest potential weekly income for holders.

But the fund could run into some issues today. Notice in the chart below that ARKK's implied volatility has declined to just 33%, which is almost a 52-week low. So, there may be less dividend juice to squeeze out, so to speak. Today's landscape is a far cry from what we saw in March, April, and May, when implied volatility was quite high.

Volatility View: ARKK Implied Volatility Has Dropped Sharply

Zooming in on the equity positions, the Morningstar style box backs up my assertion that this ultra-income portfolio is linked to ARKK-like stocks. There's just 4% value exposure, with more than 80% of the total holdings (on a market cap weighted basis) classified as growth.

Shareholders are riding the high-tech wave with some income (call selling) and downside protection (put buying) guardrails. Of course, that complex structure comes at a very high annual cost.

Moreover, the dividend payouts are potentially a heavy tax hit, so I encourage investors to consider owning ULTY in a tax-sheltered account (such as an IRA or Health Savings Account).

ULTY: Portfolio Profile

Seeking Alpha notes that more than half of ULTY's equity positions are classified as being in the Information Technology sector. That helps to explain its strong recent returns, along with the high call prices from earlier this year. Moreover, the stock market rally since the April 7 intraday low has been driven by steep climbs in lower-quality, high-growth stocks.

ULTY: Holdings & Dividend Information

Key risks include sudden weakness in high-growth, SMID-cap tech stocks, as well as lower volatility levels. Its concentration compared to a more diversified option-income ETF also presents the risk that ULTY's equity basket could underperform even as, say, the Nasdaq 100 rallies.

Longer-term, the extremely high annual cost may erode net returns to shareholders, particularly those who own the fund in a taxable account.

Finally, average daily volume is high at 32 million shares, but I'd call out that its median 30-day bid/ask spread is elevated at 18 basis points. So, using limit orders is a prudent approach to trading ULTY.

The Bottom Line

I have a hold rating on ULTY. The conditions have been conducive for strong total returns since April, but it's hard to see that lasting. Lower volatility levels and a broadening out of the market rally could cool relative returns. The complex and somewhat risky strategy should by no means be seen as a fixed-income replacement.

data.title: Northern Oil and Gas: Capex Reduction Improves Near-Term Free Cash Flow
data.description:
Northern Oil and Gas (NYSE:NOG) has reduced its capex budget by around $125 million to $150 million, which should noticeably help its 2025 free cash flow. There is expected to be only a modest impact on 2025 production, although that likely puts Northern's prior aim of double-digit 2026 production growth out of reach.

Northern also received a legal settlement that gives it $48.6 million in cash. I've slightly increased my estimate of Northern's value from $32 per share to $33 per share to reflect that settlement as well as improved oil prices since I last looked at Northern.

Convertible Notes

Northern issued another $200 million in 3.625% convertible unsecured notes due April 2029. It issued these notes at a premium (105.597%) to par, allowing it to raise approximately $211.2 million in gross proceeds. This became approximately $203.6 million in net proceeds after debt issuance costs.

The conversion price for the convertible notes is nominally around $36.87, but Northern spent $16.9 million to enter into capped call transactions that increases the effective conversion price of the notes to $50.8709.

It also spent $35 million to repurchase 1.1 million shares at an average price of $31.15 per share.

This left it with approximately $152 million in remaining funds. Due to the share repurchases and the capped call transactions, the potential net dilution from these transactions appears to be around 2.8 million shares if the additional $200 million in notes are eventually converted into shares.

It also noted that it is saving around $5 million per year on interest and dividends from the share repurchases and the lower interest rate on its convertible notes compared to its credit facility debt.

Northern now has $700 million in 3.625% convertible unsecured notes due April 2029.

Q2 2025 Results

Northern reported 134,094 BOEPD in Q2 2025 production, including oil volumes of 76,944 barrels per day. This was a roughly 1% quarter-over-quarter decline in total production and a 2% quarter-over-quarter decline in oil production.

Lower activity in the Williston Basin contributed to the modest decline in oil production. On the other hand, Northern reported record Appalachian volumes (contributing to its record natural gas volumes), while its Uinta Basin volumes increased by over 18.5% quarter-over-quarter, after a 15+% quarter-over-quarter increase in Q1 2025.

Northern generated another $126 million in free cash flow in Q2 2025, around 7% lower than Q1 2025, with the decline largely due to weaker commodity prices. Northern has a solid hedging position that contributed $61 million to its Q2 2025 free cash flow.

Changes To Full Year Guidance

Northern has reduced its 2025 capex budget by $138 million at guidance midpoint. It is reducing its Permian Basin and Williston Basin spending by an estimated $180 million and $60 million respectively, while increasing its Appalachian and Uinta Basin spending by around $75 million and $25 million respectively.

Northern has trimmed its full year 2025 total production guidance by 1,000 BOEPD and its oil production guidance by 2,000 barrels per day. Northern's natural gas production is helped by the increased Appalachian spending.

In general, Northern's development changes have a limited effect on its 2025 production. The impact will be felt more in 2026. It was planning on double-digit production growth in 2026 before, but may end up much closer to maintenance level now, depending on commodity prices.

Northern made some tweaks to its cost guidance too, expecting slightly higher lease operating expenses (per BOE) and slightly wider oil differentials. However, it also expects a lower production tax percentage, so the overall effect is very close to neutral.

2H 2025 Outlook

Based on its full year guidance midpoint, Northern expects approximately 128,500 BOEPD in 2H 2025 production, including 72,250 barrels per day in oil production.

The current strip for the second half of the year involves roughly $63 WTI oil and $3.15 Henry Hub natural gas. At those commodity prices, Northern may generate $1.028 billion in revenues after hedges (with its hedges contributing $99 million in positive value).

Barrels/Mcf

Northern's capex (excluding non-budgeted acquisitions) may be around $525 million during the second half of the year. Despite the capex reductions, this is still 14% higher than its 1H 2025 capex, as Q2 2025 was a light capex quarter.

Northern may be able to generate $110 million in free cash flow during the second half of 2025.

This is lower than its Q2 2025 free cash flow as it is affected by the higher proportion of capex coming in the second half of the year.

Notes On Valuation

I had estimated Northern's value at $32 per share based on long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and $3.75 Henry Hub natural gas, but I am increasing my estimate of Northern's value slightly to $33 per share now.

Northern expects to receive a cash settlement of $48.6 million (net of legal settlement expenses) in Q3 2025. This is related to a settlement with a North Dakota operator around post-production costs that were deducted from revenues. This settlement is for close to $0.50 per share in cash. As well, near-term oil strip prices have improved a bit since I last looked at Northern.

Northern's production growth in 2026 may be relatively low now, but its free cash flow would benefit from slower growth. It mentioned an $850 million capex budget to maintain oil production levels. Capital efficiency could improve a bit as well as Northern noted that it expects its operating partners to focus more on core inventory with lower prices, improving new well productivity.

Conclusion

Northern Oil and Gas has reduced its capex budget for 2025 and may generate around $110 million in free cash flow during the second half of the year. It previously was on track for heavy spending late in 2025 to set itself up for strong production growth in 2026.

Given a low-$60s current oil price, I think it makes sense to reduce production growth. I still am maintaining expectations for long-term (after 2026) $70 WTI oil and estimate Northern's value at $33 per share in that scenario.

data.title: A Case For Cuts? What To Expect From The U.S. Fed
data.description:
Markets continue to hit record highs as investors appear to be counting on the U.S. Federal Reserve to cut interest rates next week. But new inflation data could make the central bank cautious. Alexandra Gorewicz, VP, Director and Portfolio Manager, TD Asset Management, joins MoneyTalk to break down the Fed’s conundrum.

Transcript

Greg Bonnell - Pace of US inflation actually accelerated in August. But does that mean anything when it comes to a Fed rate decision and an expectation of a cut? Widely expected. Joining us now to discuss is Alex Gorewicz, VP, director, and portfolio manager for Active Fixed Income at TD Asset Management. Alex, welcome back to the program.

Alexandra Gorewicz - Thanks, Greg. Great to be here.

Greg Bonnell - So we did get that print today. And it was within expectations. But it is a move higher. I think the highest inflation has been in the States on a headline basis since January, right before a Fed decision. How does this all go into the calculus? What's your take on the CPI number?

Alexandra Gorewicz - So when we look at the month-over-month print, which came in somewhere between 0.3% and 0.4% month-over-month or the annual print, whether we're talking about headline or core inflation, anywhere between 2.9% and 3.1%, those numbers - not only are they broadly in line with expectations from market strategists or market pundits but also reinforce the pass-through to PCE, which is the inflation metric that the Fed cares about - is going to be plus or minus in line with their expectations.

And part of that is a combination of some - we'll call it one-off surprises - for example, food popped higher month over month, unlike prior months, where it was a lot more benign or even a drag, whereas some tariff-related price pressures are continuing or even firming up further - for example, new or used cars. But nothing material or standout that would worry the Fed that the way inflation is materializing deviates from their forecast.

Greg Bonnell - So nothing to worry the Fed on inflation. We did get the weekly jobless claims. And I believe they're at their highest in quite a period, too. Of course, they have a dual mandate. So when we take a look at the state of the US labor market right now, particularly after the jobs Friday report, this seems to be signs of caution.

Alexandra Gorewicz - Correct. And that's what, ultimately, the market reacted to a lot more. There was actually a tiny delay in that claims data release right after CPI. So on CPI, if you looked at the bond market, it was neither here nor there. And then as soon as that claims data came out, we saw bond yields fall across the yield curve in the US, and actually Canada, as well, but more of a move in sympathy.

On claims data or the jobs picture in general, today's data probably comes with a little bit of an asterisk in that the floods, unfortunate floods, that hit Texas, that happened in Texas in the last couple of months - there was a deadline at the beginning of September to apply for effectively, disaster relief, if you will. So you did see a tick-up in claims, particularly in Texas. The question is, how does that persist over the next couple of weeks, so the market will be watching that closely?

But then to your point about Friday's jobs data, we've seen, whether it's been through revisions, which is what we had on Friday, or whether it's been through just the broader trend in the last couple of months, more softening than expected. I wouldn't call them cracks. I wouldn't call them -- definitely, I wouldn't call them a collapse, but certainly a lot more softening than expected in the labor picture.

Greg Bonnell - So the Fed has this inflation report. It has this picture of the labor market. It walks into that two-day meeting next week with all those documents on the show. What do they make of it all? What happens around that table?

Alexandra Gorewicz - So when I think about the Fed, there are a couple of things to note for next week. Because it is the September meeting, we will get their updated forecasts. And that's important because that also comes with updated dot plots. So what are they thinking about where monetary policy will go for the next several meetings and next several years?

So what the expectation right now from the market is relative to their last dot plot, which was in June and showed two rate cuts to the end of the year. I think the market has moved towards expecting three rate cuts. In other words, September, October, and December, from the market's point of view, will all be rate cuts from the Fed. We'll see if the Fed actually matches market expectations. But history tells us that they probably will. That's generally what happens. The market moves in a direction. And the Fed tends to follow suit.

But also, just looking at the commentary or the concerns from some of the Fed members, particularly around the labor picture, those concerns seem to be validated by, again, the weaker-than-expected data points. What this means to me is that there is a risk that it won't just be one or two Fed members that might dissent for a 50-basis point rate cut. You could end up getting maybe three or four. If you get more than two, I don't think the bond market is expecting that. And it would move towards lower bond yields than what we've currently seen.

Greg Bonnell - Could you justify a 50 in this environment?

Alexandra Gorewicz - Not from the data that we've seen, not in my opinion, anyway. Where I do see a lot more conviction - and again, it's really just in line with what the bond market is saying - is a 25-basis point rate cut at each of the last three meetings, so next week and then in October and December. But if we see more dissents or some level of dissent towards a 50 cut in September, I think you would see a pretty strong bond market reaction.

Greg Bonnell - Let's take it to the bond market reaction, ultimately what all this means for the fixed income investor. What does it mean?

Alexandra Gorewicz - It means that the market has correctly reacted to the softening of the labor data. Again, inflation-related data, whether we're talking about PPI, CPI, even the expected PCE that we're going to get in the next couple of weeks, all of the inflation-level data is showing tariff-related price pressures but not egregious ones.

So they're coming in line with expectations, whereas what is missing expectations is the labor side. It's the jobs data. It's the picture around the job market that is going to -- or that is expected to put the Fed on a more dovish path. The market has already moved in that direction. But again, any additional dovishness relative to that market expectation should see rates continue their more recent trend over the last one to two months, which is towards lower bond yields.

Original Post

data.title: Another Strong Week For Stocks
data.description:
With the week now complete, below is a helpful look at the recent performance of various asset classes using key ETFs traded in the US.

Large-cap growth (IVW) was up 2.6% on the week, while mid-caps and small-caps actually fell. Technology (XLK) was the best-performing sector due to Oracle's (ORCL) massive gain, while Consumer Staples (XLP) and Materials (XLB) were the worst.

Outside of the US, there are a dozen country ETFs now up 20%+ on the year, which easily beats out SPY's year-to-date gain of 12.8%. Spain (EWP) is up the most in 2025 with a huge gain of 59.6%.

Argentina (ARGT) and natural gas (UNG) were the two worst-performing ETFs in our matrix this week. Sticking with commodities, agriculture (DBA), gold (GLD), and silver (SLV) all rallied more than 1%, with GLD and SLV extending huge year-to-date gains.

Finally, Treasury ETFs were also up across the board this week as rates fell, especially at the long end.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Consumer Sentiment Falls To 4-Month Low In September
data.description:
By Jennifer Nash

Consumer sentiment fell for a second straight month, hitting its lowest level since May. The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index was down 2.8 points, or about 5%, to 55.4 in September. The latest reading was lower than the forecast of 58.2. Consumer sentiment has fallen by 21.0% (14.7 points) compared to a year ago.

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index is a monthly survey of consumer confidence levels in the U.S. with regard to the economy, personal finances, business conditions, and buying conditions, conducted by the University of Michigan. There are two reports released each month: a preliminary report released mid-month and a final report released at the end of the month.

Joanne Hsu, the director of surveys, made the following comments:

Consumer sentiment moved down less than three index points in early September. This month’s easing in economic views was particularly strong among lower and middle income consumers. Buying conditions for durables improved, while all other index components fell. Consumers continue to note multiple vulnerabilities in the economy, with rising risks to business conditions, labor markets, and inflation. Likewise, consumers perceive risks to their pocketbooks as well; current and expected personal finances both eased about 8% this month. Trade policy remains highly salient to consumers, with about 60% of consumers providing unprompted comments about tariffs during interviews, little changed from last month. Still, sentiment remains above April and May 2025 readings, immediately after the initial announcement of reciprocal tariffs.

See the chart below for a long-term perspective on this widely watched indicator. We've highlighted the index's value at the start of each recession and included a callout to the most recent 12 months. At 55.4, the current level is below the index's value at the start of all six recessions since its inception.

To put today’s report in historical context, consumer sentiment is currently 34.3% below its average reading of 84.3 (arithmetic mean) and 33.4% below its geometric mean of 83.1, based on data dating back to 1978. The current index level is at the 1st percentile of the 573 monthly data points in this series.

This indicator is somewhat volatile, with an average monthly change of 3.1 points. The latest data shows a 2.8-point change from the previous month. To visualize the volatility, here’s a chart with monthly data and a three-month moving average.

The bottom half of the chart includes real GDP, which allows us to evaluate the correlation between the Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index and the broader economy.

Each month, the survey results highlight sentiment within each political party. To provide additional context, I’ve included a chart that shows the presidencies over our timeframe. As you can see, sentiment has fluctuated both positively and negatively under both Republican and Democratic administrations.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Components

The Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index consists of two sub-indexes: the Current Economic Conditions Index (CECI) and the Consumer Expectations Index (CEI). The CECI reflects consumers' views of their current financial situation and the overall economy, while the CEI gauges their outlook for the future.

In September, the CECI fell for a second straight month to 61.2, its lowest level since May. This represents a 0.8% decline from the previous month and a 3.3% drop from a year ago. This reading was below the forecast of 61.3.

Meanwhile, the CEI fell for a third straight month to 51.8, its lowest level since May. This represents a 7.3% decline from the previous month and a 30.4% drop from one year ago. This reading was below the forecast of 54.9.

Michigan Consumer Sentiment Index: Inflation Expectations

Year-ahead inflation expectations held steady at 4.8%, unchanged from August. Long-run inflation expectations moved up for the second straight month to 3.9% in September. This current reading is considerably lower than the 4.4% seen in April.

Other Sentiment Indicators

For an additional perspective on consumer attitudes, see the most recent Conference Board's Consumer Confidence Index. Both indexes gauge consumer attitudes toward the current and future strength of the economy. However, the Consumer Confidence Index is more influenced by employment and labor market conditions, while the Michigan Sentiment Index is more focused on household finances and the impact of inflation.

The Conference Board index is the more volatile of the two, but the broad pattern and general trends have been remarkably similar to the Michigan index.

And finally, the prevailing mood of the Michigan survey is also similar to the mood of small business owners, as captured by the NFIB business optimism Index (monthly update here).

ETFs associated with sentiment include the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLY).

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: WTI Crude Oil Slips Below $62 As Supply Concerns Outweigh Demand Optimism
data.description:
By Jainam Mehta

​WTI crude oil futures are facing renewed pressure, sliding below $62 per barrel in Friday’s session and extending losses for a third day. The downturn comes after U.S. government data showed a surprise inventory build of 3.9 million barrels, defying expectations for a draw and raising doubts about the strength of domestic demand.

Global supply expectations have added to the bearish tone. The International Energy Agency projected stronger-than-anticipated output growth, largely from OPEC+, while OPEC itself left demand projections steady. The divergence highlights the uncertainty over whether markets can absorb additional flows without tipping into surplus.

Geopolitical factors remain in play. Washington is pressing G7 allies to impose harsher tariffs on India and China for their purchases of Russian crude, a move that could alter trade dynamics. Still, near-term balances remain dictated by inventory data and the pace of U.S. consumption, which has softened as refining margins narrow and gasoline demand cools after the summer peak.

Technical levels point to fragility

From a technical standpoint, WTI has broken down from its short-lived ascending channel that lifted prices earlier in September. The retreat through $63.50 and $63.10 has turned those levels into resistance.

Moving averages have rolled over, with the 20-, 50-, and 100-period EMAs now stacked overhead, reinforcing a bearish structure. The RSI at 43 shows momentum is weak but not yet oversold, leaving scope for additional declines.

Immediate support lies at $61.50, with the psychological $60 mark viewed as the critical level to maintain. A rebound above $63.50–$64.10 would be required to restore bullish momentum, while failure to defend $61 could open the path toward $59–$60.

Outlook

Despite this week’s weakness, WTI is still holding onto modest weekly gains, with earlier strength driven by geopolitical risks in the Middle East and Ukraine providing a floor. Yet unless disruptions escalate, the balance of risks remains tilted lower, with supply growth and fading U.S. demand overshadowing bullish catalysts.

In our previous analysis, we highlighted how WTI’s attempts to reclaim the mid-$60s were fragile without clear evidence of stronger consumption. The current breakdown validates that caution, placing greater importance on whether $60 can hold as the market’s next test.

This material may contain third-party opinions, none of the data and information on this webpage constitutes investment advice according to our Disclaimer. While we adhere to strict Editorial Integrity, this post may contain references to products from our partners.

Original Post
